5.2.equation: To compute the OTOC, we do the analytic continuation of the following TO correlator, \langle B † (z1 , z̄1 )B(z2 , z̄2 )A† (z3 , z̄3 )A(z4 , z̄4 )\rangle\beta , so that the operator ordering becomes \langle B † A† (t)B A(t)\rangle\beta . This can be obtained by the following choice of coordinates on the thermal cylinder as [63], 2\pi 2\pi z1 = e \beta (t+iε1 ) , z̄1 = e- \beta (t+iε1 ) , 2\pi 2\pi z2 = e \beta (t+iε2 ) , z̄2 = e- \beta (t+iε1 ) , 2\pi 2\pi z3 = e \beta (x+iε3 ) , z̄3 = e+ \beta (x-iε3 )

5.3.equation: where we analytically continued the correlator to the real-time considering ε1 < ε3 < ε2 < ε4 . At late times, the OTOC is exponentially damped for truly chaotic systems, and it may decay polynomically for non-chaotic models. The time evolution of the cross-ratio z is given by (1-z) \to (1 - z)e-2\pii , which means, in turn, that the late time behaviour of the OTOC is given by the corresponding Regge limit of the correlator. In this case, the cross-ratio on the complex plane is given by, -2\pi -2\pi z ≈ e \beta (x-t) ε∗12 ε34 , z̄ ≈ -e \beta (x+t) ε∗12 ε34
